title: Setup
text: Please complete these steps before the tutorial begins. If you run into trouble, find us before the session starts or post in the workshop chat.

title: Setup
text:
Python Environment
Pick one of the following methods — they all produce the same result.
Here are the packages you’ll be installing:


requirements.txt

altair
ibis-framework[duckdb,examples,polars]
narwhals
polars
anthropic
anthropic[bedrock]
boto3
chatlas
faicons
ipykernel
jupyter
langchain
langchain-anthropic
langchain-openai
langgraph
openai
palmerpenguins
pandas
pillow
playwright
plotly
plotnine
python-dotenv
querychat
requests
ridgeplot
seaborn
shiny
shinychat
shinylive
shinywidgets
tokenizers



python + venvcondauvpixi


python -m venv .venv

# macOS / Linux
source .venv/bin/activate

# Windows
.venv\Scripts\activate

pip install -r requirements.txt


conda create -n pycon2026 python=3.12
conda activate pycon2026
pip install -r requirements.txt


uv venv .venv

# macOS / Linux
source .venv/bin/activate

# Windows
.venv\Scripts\activate

uv pip install -r requirements.txt


pixi init .
pixi add python==3.12.* pip
pixi run python -m pip install -r requirements.txt
pixi shell

title: Setup
text:
IDE
We recommend Positron, this is what the workshop will be using. VS Code works just as well.
Whichever you choose, install the Shiny extension (it works in both).






Note



We will not be using Jupyter Notebooks during this workshop.

title: Setup
text:
AI / Chat Model

GitHub Models (free, recommended)
You’ll need a GitHub Personal Access Token (PAT). It doesn’t require any special scopes.

Create a classic PAT following GitHub’s instructions
Set the token as an environment variable:

export GITHUB_TOKEN=your_token_here   # macOS / Linux
$env:GITHUB_TOKEN="your_token_here"   # Windows PowerShell


Local LLM via Ollama (optional, no account needed)

Download and install Ollama
Pull a model that fits your machine:




Model
Download Size
Install Command




qwen3:0.6b
523 MB
ollama run qwen3:0.6b


phi4-mini-reasoning
3.2 GB
ollama run phi4-mini-reasoning


qwen3
5.2 GB
ollama run qwen3


devstral
14 GB
ollama run devstral





Paid API (optional)






Note



Consumer subscriptions (Claude.ai, ChatGPT Plus, etc.) and API access are billed separately.



Anthropic ClaudeOpenAIGoogle Gemini



Sign up at https://console.anthropic.com
Add credits
Create an API key at https://console.anthropic.com/settings/keys

export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=your_key_here



Sign up at https://openai.com/
Create an API key at https://platform.openai.com/api-keys

export OPENAI_API_KEY=your_key_here



Sign in at https://aistudio.google.com
Click Create API key

export GOOGLE_API_KEY=your_key_here

title: Setup
text:
Verify Your Installation

Clone or download this repository: https://github.com/chendaniely/pycon-2026-shiny
Activate your Python environment

Step 1 — check that all packages import correctly:
python test-install.py
You should see a ✓ next to every package and a final All good! message.
Step 2 — check that Shiny is working:
shiny run test-install.py
You should see:
INFO:     Started server process [...]
INFO:     Waiting for application startup.
INFO:     Application startup complete.
INFO:     Uvicorn running on http://127.0.0.1:8000 (Press CTRL+C to quit)
Open http://127.0.0.1:8000 in your browser and confirm the page loads.

title: AI + LLM Dashboards
text: View slides in full screen

title: AI + LLM Dashboards
text:
Putting LLMs in Shiny apps
Three patterns, increasing complexity:

Chat interface — a conversational UI (shinychat)
Data chat — query your dataframe with natural language (querychat)
Tool calling — let the LLM take actions in your app

title: AI + LLM Dashboards
text:
shinychat
shinychat provides a ready-made chat UI component for Shiny:
from shiny.express import ui
from shinychat import chat_ui, chat_server
from chatlas import ChatGithub

ui.page_opts(title="My Chatbot")

chat_ui("chat")

@chat_server("chat")
def _():
    return ChatGithub(model="gpt-4.1", system_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.")
Docs: https://posit-dev.github.io/shinychat/py/

title: AI + LLM Dashboards
AI + LLM Dashboards
text:
Chatting with your data
What if users could ask questions about your dataset in plain English?
querychat translates natural language → SQL → runs against a DuckDB table:
import querychat
import seaborn as sns

tips = sns.load_dataset("tips")
qc = querychat.init(tips, "tips")
Docs: https://github.com/posit-dev/querychat

title: AI + LLM Dashboards
text:
Demo: querychat
import querychat
import seaborn as sns
from shiny.express import ui

tips = sns.load_dataset("tips")
qc = querychat.init(tips, "tips",
                    greeting="Ask me about the tips dataset!")

ui.page_opts(title="Tips Q&A", fillable=True)
querychat.chat_ui("chat")
querychat.dataframe_ui("df")

querychat.chat_server("chat", qc)
querychat.dataframe_server("df", qc)

title: AI + LLM Dashboards
text:
Your turn






TipExercise (~15 min)




Run the shinychat example and chat with it
Modify the system prompt to make it an expert on a specific topic
Try the querychat example with a dataset of your choice
Stretch: swap the model to an Ollama local model and compare responses

title: AI + LLM Dashboards
text:
Tool calling
LLMs can call functions in your code:
def get_weather(city: str) -> str:
    """Get the current weather for a city."""
    ...  # call a weather API

chat = ChatGithub(model="gpt-4.1")
chat.register_tool(get_weather)
chat.chat("What's the weather in Long Beach?")
The LLM decides when to call the tool and what arguments to pass.

title: AI + LLM Dashboards
text:
Sidebot pattern
Use an LLM to drive filters in a dashboard via natural language:

User types “show me only lunch orders over $20”
LLM translates to filter parameters
Dashboard updates reactively

Template: shiny create --mode core --github jcheng5/py-databot

title: An AI + LLM Primer
text: View slides in full screen

title: An AI + LLM Primer
text:
Before we start






Warning



DO NOT send proprietary code, credentials, or sensitive data to any LLM unless you are certain your organization’s IT policies permit it.
Local models (Ollama) stay on your machine.

title: An AI + LLM Primer
text:
Have you used…


ChatGPT?
Claude?
Gemini?
A local model via Ollama?

title: An AI + LLM Primer
text:
What we’ll cover
We’ll treat LLMs as black boxes — you send text in, text comes back.
Goal: demystify the API so you can wire one into a Shiny app.

title: An AI + LLM Primer
text:
LLM conversations are HTTP requests


Each interaction is a separate HTTP API call
The server is entirely stateless
“Memory” of past messages is simulated by resending the entire history each time

title: An AI + LLM Primer
text:
Example conversation

“What’s the capital of the moon?”

. . .
"There isn't one."
. . .

“Are you sure?”

. . .
"Yes, I am sure."

title: An AI + LLM Primer
text:
Example request
curl https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $OPENAI_API_KEY" \
  -d '{
    "model": "gpt-4.1",
    "messages": [
      {"role": "system", "content": "You are a terse assistant."},
      {"role": "user",   "content": "What is the capital of the moon?"}
    ]
  }'

model: which model to use
system: behind-the-scenes instructions
user: the user’s message

title: An AI + LLM Primer
An AI + LLM Primer
section: Follow-up request: full history is resent
text:
Follow-up request: full history is resent
  "messages": [
    {"role": "system",    "content": "You are a terse assistant."},
    {"role": "user",      "content": "What is the capital of the moon?"},
    {"role": "assistant", "content": "The moon does not have a capital."},
    {"role": "user",      "content": "Are you sure?"}
  ]
The entire conversation is replayed on every request.

title: An AI + LLM Primer
text:
Tokens
Tokens are the fundamental unit — roughly ¾ of a word on average.

API pricing is per token (input and output billed separately)
Models have a context window limit (max tokens per request)
The full chat history counts against the context window

Try it: https://tiktokenizer.vercel.app/

title: An AI + LLM Primer
text:
Providers



Provider
Models
Notes




OpenAI
GPT-4.1, o3
OPENAI_API_KEY


Anthropic
Claude Sonnet/Opus
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY


Google
Gemini 2.5
GOOGLE_API_KEY


GitHub Models
Many (free)
GITHUB_TOKEN


Ollama
Llama, Qwen, …
Local, free

title: An AI + LLM Primer
text:
GitHub Models (recommended for today)
Free access to many frontier models using a GitHub PAT.
export GITHUB_TOKEN=your_personal_access_token
Docs: https://docs.github.com/en/github-models

title: An AI + LLM Primer
text:
chatlas
chatlas is a unified Python interface to all major LLM providers:
from chatlas import ChatOpenAI, ChatAnthropic, ChatGoogleGemini, ChatGithub

chat = ChatGithub(model="gpt-4.1")
chat.chat("What is the capital of France?")
Same API regardless of provider — swap one line to switch models.

title: An AI + LLM Primer
text:
Your turn






TipExercise (~10 min)




Set your GITHUB_TOKEN environment variable
Use chatlas to ask an LLM a question from a Python script
Try switching to a different model or provider

title: Wrap Up
text: View slides in full screen

title: Wrap Up
text:
What we covered

Shiny’s input → output → reactivity model
Building and running your first app
@reactive.calc for shared computations
Dashboard layouts: sidebars, columns, cards, value boxes
Deploying to Posit Connect Cloud
Integrating LLMs with chatlas, shinychat, and querychat
Testing with Playwright

title: Wrap Up
text:
Key concepts to take home

Reactivity is automatic — Shiny tracks dependencies for you
Start static — get the visualization right, then add inputs
@reactive.calc — avoid redundant computation across outputs
requirements.txt — always needed for deployment
SQL is a great intermediate language between humans and LLMs

title: Wrap Up
text:
This workshop
Everything from today:
https://github.com/chendaniely/pycon-2026-shiny

text:
Feedback survey
Please take 2 minutes to fill out the PyCon session feedback — it directly influences future tutorial selections.
Survey link is in your email / QR Code

title: Testing Shiny Apps
text: View slides in full screen

title: Testing Shiny Apps
text:
Why test?

Manual “click around and check” doesn’t scale
Catch regressions before users do
Document expected behavior as executable code

title: Testing Shiny Apps
text:
Playwright
Playwright is a modern browser automation library.
Shiny provides a Playwright controller that understands Shiny-specific widgets:
from playwright.sync_api import Page
from shiny.playwright import controller
from shiny.run import ShinyAppProc
Docs: https://shiny.posit.co/py/docs/testing.html

title: Testing Shiny Apps
text:
Test structure
from playwright.sync_api import Page
from shiny.playwright import controller
from shiny.run import ShinyAppProc

def test_species_filter(page: Page, local_app: ShinyAppProc):
    page.goto(local_app.url)

    radio = controller.InputRadioButtons(page, "species")
    plot  = controller.OutputPlot(page, "plot")

    radio.expect_choices(["Adelie", "Chinstrap", "Gentoo"])
    radio.set("Chinstrap")
    plot.expect_img_src(timeout=3000)  # plot re-rendered

title: Testing Shiny Apps
text:
pytest fixtures
# conftest.py
import pytest
from shiny.run import run_shiny_app

@pytest.fixture(scope="module")
def local_app():
    with run_shiny_app("app.py") as proc:
        yield proc
Run with:
pytest tests/

title: Testing Shiny Apps
text:
Controllers
Shiny provides typed controllers for every input and output type:
# Inputs
controller.InputRadioButtons(page, "id")
controller.InputSlider(page, "id")
controller.InputSelect(page, "id")
controller.InputCheckboxGroup(page, "id")

# Outputs
controller.OutputPlot(page, "id")
controller.OutputText(page, "id")
controller.OutputDataFrame(page, "id")

title: Testing Shiny Apps
text:
What to test

Default state renders without errors
Each input change produces the expected output change
Edge cases: empty selections, out-of-range values
Error messages appear when they should

title: Testing Shiny Apps
text:
Your turn






TipExercise (~10 min)




Install Playwright: pip install playwright && playwright install chromium
Write a test for your first app that:

Checks the default radio button selection
Changes the selection and verifies the plot updates

Run pytest and watch it pass

Today’s goal
Learn how to build reactive web apps with Shiny for Python, from the simple to the mission critical.

Python?

Interactive dashboards (Dash, Streamlit, Panel, …)?

title: Your First Application
text:
Development approach
Start static, then add interactivity.

Write the visualization you want
Identify what the user should be able to change
Wrap inputs around those variables
Connect inputs to outputs

title: Your First Application
text:
Step 1 — static plot
from palmerpenguins import load_penguins
from plotnine import aes, geom_point, ggplot, theme_minimal

dat = load_penguins().dropna()

(
    ggplot(dat, aes(x="bill_depth_mm", y="body_mass_g", color="species"))
    + geom_point(alpha=0.7)
    + theme_minimal()
)

Step 4 — add inputs
from palmerpenguins import load_penguins
from plotnine import aes, geom_point, ggplot, theme_minimal
from shiny.express import input, render, ui

dat = load_penguins().dropna()

title: Your First Application
text:
Step 5 — make the output reactive
from palmerpenguins import load_penguins
from plotnine import aes, geom_point, ggplot, theme_minimal
from shiny.express import input, render, ui

dat = load_penguins().dropna()
num_cols = dat.select_dtypes("float64").columns.tolist()

ui.input_select("x", "", num_cols, selected="bill_depth_mm")
ui.input_select("y", "", num_cols, selected="body_mass_g")


1@render.plot
def plot():
    return (
2        ggplot(dat, aes(x=input.x(), y=input.y(), color="species"))
        + geom_point(alpha=0.7)
        + theme_minimal()
    )

1

@render.plot tells Shiny this function produces a plot

2

input.x() and input.y() read the current dropdown selections

title: Your First Application
text:
What just happened?

input.x() and input.y() are reactive values — they change when the user picks
@render.plot creates a reactive context — it re-runs whenever its inputs change
Both dropdowns drive the same output with no extra wiring

Shiny tracked both dependencies automatically.

title: Your First Application
text:
The complete app


code/app-01-intro.py

from palmerpenguins import load_penguins
from plotnine import aes, geom_point, ggplot, theme_minimal
from shiny.express import input, render, ui

dat = load_penguins().dropna()
num_cols = dat.select_dtypes("float64").columns.tolist()

ui.input_select("x", "", num_cols, selected="bill_depth_mm")
ui.input_select("y", "", num_cols, selected="body_mass_g")


@render.plot
def plot():
    return (ggplot(dat, aes(x=input.x(), y=input.y(), color="species")) +
        geom_point(alpha=0.7) +
        theme_minimal()
    )

Open code/app-01-intro.py and run it.

Add a third input — a checkbox group to filter by species — and connect it to the plot
Try ui.input_slider() to filter by one of the numeric columns
Add a @render.text output showing how many points are currently displayed

title: Shiny Reactivity
text:
References

Gordon Shotwell & Tracy Teal — Build Simple and Scalable Apps with Shiny | PyData NYC 2023
Joe Cheng — Shiny: Data-centric web applications in Python | PyData Seattle 2023

title: Shiny Reactivity
text:
What makes Shiny special?
Reactivity.

Outputs automatically update whenever their inputs change — without explicit event handling or manual refreshes.


No writing callbacks
No polling
Shiny tracks dependencies for you

title: Shiny Reactivity
text:
The reactive graph
Every Shiny app has a reactive graph:
input.species()  ──►  plot()
input.bins()     ──►  plot()
                 ──►  summary()
When an input changes, only the outputs that depend on it re-execute.

title: Shiny Reactivity
Shiny Reactivity
Shiny Reactivity
text:
@reactive.calc in the graph
input.species()  ──►  filtered()  ──►  plot()
                                   ──►  count()
filtered() runs once. Both outputs read from the cache.

title: Shiny Reactivity
Shiny Reactivity
text:
Reactive rules

Read a reactive value inside a reactive context → dependency is tracked
Write a reactive value → invalidates all dependents
Outside a reactive context → reading a reactive value raises an error

title: Shiny Reactivity
text:
When to use what







Need
Tool




Filter / transform data shared by multiple outputs
@reactive.calc


Respond to a button or event
@reactive.effect


Store mutable state
reactive.value


Render output
@render.plot / @render.text / …

title: Shiny Reactivity
text:
Your turn






Exercise (~15 min)


In your app from the previous exercise:

Extract the data filtering step into a @reactive.calc
Use it in both a plot and a text output showing the filtered row count
Add an action button that resets the species selection

title: Deploying Your Application
text:
Options



Platform
Cost
Notes




Posit Connect Cloud
Free tier
Easiest, GitHub-based deploy


shinyapps.io
Free tier
Classic option, rsconnect CLI


Posit Connect (self-hosted)
Paid
Enterprise, on-prem


Shinylive
Free
Runs entirely in the browser (no server)

title: Deploying Your Application
text:
requirements.txt is required
Posit Connect Cloud installs packages from requirements.txt before running your app.


requirements.txt

shiny
palmerpenguins
plotnine
pandas

If a package isn’t listed, the deployed app will crash with an ImportError.

title: Deploying Your Application
text:
Deploy to Posit Connect Cloud

Go to https://connect.posit.cloud and sign in with GitHub
Click New Content → Import from GitHub
Select your repository and branch
Set the Entry point to your app.py
Click Deploy

That’s it. Posit Connect Cloud handles the environment.

title: Deploying Your Application
text:
Deploy with the publish button
In Positron or VS Code with the Shiny extension:

Open your app.py
Click the Publish button in the editor toolbar
Follow the prompts to connect your account

title: Deploying Your Application
text:
Updating a deployed app
Push to the connected branch — Connect Cloud redeploys automatically.
Or click Redeploy in the Connect Cloud dashboard.

title: Deploying Your Application
text:
Shinylive: no server required
Compile your app to WebAssembly and host it as a static site:
shinylive export app/ site/
Limitations:

No file I/O, no database connections
Package support is limited to what Pyodide supports
Startup is slower (downloads Python + packages to browser)

Great for: demos, tutorials, sharing one-off apps.

title: Deploying Your Application
text:
Your turn






Exercise (~5 min)



Make sure your app has a requirements.txt
Push it to GitHub
Deploy it to Posit Connect Cloud

title: Introduction to Shiny
text:
What is a Shiny app?
Each Shiny app involves:

A web page that users interact with
A Python process that watches the page and responds to user events

The Python process runs your code and sends results back to the browser.

title: Introduction to Shiny
text:
Components
Each Shiny app consists of:

Inputs — widgets the user can interact with (sliders, dropdowns, checkboxes, …)
Outputs — things Shiny renders and displays (plots, tables, text, …)
Instructions — your code that builds outputs from inputs

title: Introduction to Shiny
text:
Reactivity
When an input changes, Shiny reacts by rebuilding the outputs that depend on it — and only those outputs.
No callbacks. No manual refresh. No cache management.

title: Introduction to Shiny
text:
Spreadsheet analogy
Think of a spreadsheet:

Inputs = cells you type values into
Outputs = formula cells
Instructions = the formulas

When you change a value cell, only the formula cells that depend on it recalculate.
Shiny works the same way.

title: Introduction to Shiny
text:
Key features

Easy enough for prototypes
Efficient enough to handle complexity
Scales from a quick script to a production-ready app

title: Introduction to Shiny
text:
Why Shiny for Python?
Shiny lets you build reactive web apps without worrying about:

cache
state
callbacks
…or even HTML, CSS, and JavaScript

title: Introduction to Shiny
text:
Shiny Express vs Shiny Core






Important


Shiny for Python has two syntaxes for writing the same apps:

Shiny Express — lightweight, great for quick exploration and teaching
Shiny Core — more explicit, closer to Shiny for R




Today we’ll use Shiny Express.

title: Introduction to Shiny
text:
Running a Shiny app
shiny run app.py
Or in Positron / VS Code: click the Run App button in the editor toolbar.
Open http://127.0.0.1:8000 in your browser.

title: Your Next Application
text:
Beyond the basics
Your first app had one input and one output.
Real dashboards have:

Layout structure (sidebars, columns, tabs)
Multiple coordinated outputs
Summary statistics (value boxes)
Shared reactive computations

title: Your Next Application
text:
Layout components
from shiny.express import input, render, ui

ui.page_opts(title="Tips Dashboard", fillable=True)

with ui.sidebar():
    ui.input_slider("bill", "Max bill ($)", 3, 50, 25)
    ui.input_checkbox_group("time", "Meal", ["Lunch", "Dinner"],
                            selected=["Lunch", "Dinner"])

with ui.layout_columns():
    with ui.card():
        ...  # output 1

    with ui.card():
        ...  # output 2

title: Your Next Application
text:
Value boxes
from shiny.express import render, ui
from faicons import icon_svg

with ui.value_box(showcase=icon_svg("dollar-sign")):
    "Average bill"

    @render.text
    def avg_bill():
        return f"${filtered()['total_bill'].mean():.2f}"

title: Your Next Application
Your Next Application
text:
Full app structure
ui.page_opts(title="Tips Dashboard", fillable=True)

with ui.sidebar():
    # inputs ...

@reactive.calc
def filtered():
    # shared data transform ...

with ui.layout_columns():
    with ui.value_box(...):
        @render.text
        def avg_bill(): ...

    with ui.card():
        @render.plot
        def scatter(): ...

    with ui.card():
        @render.data_frame
        def table(): ...

title: Your Next Application
text:
Tabs and navigation
with ui.navset_card_underline():
    with ui.nav_panel("Plot"):
        @render.plot
        def scatter(): ...

    with ui.nav_panel("Table"):
        @render.data_frame
        def table(): ...

title: Your Next Application
text:
Your turn






Exercise (~25 min)


Build a dashboard using the seaborn tips dataset (or your own data):

Add a sidebar with at least two inputs
Use @reactive.calc for the filtered data
Include at least one value box, one plot, and one table
Try using ui.layout_columns() to arrange your outputs

title: Your First Application
text: View slides in full screen

title: Your First Application
text: title: “Your First Application”

title: Your First Application
text:
Development approach
Start static, then add interactivity.


Write the visualization you want
Identify what the user should be able to change
Wrap inputs around those variables
Connect inputs to outputs

title: Your First Application
text:
Step 1 — static plot
from palmerpenguins import load_penguins
from plotnine import aes, geom_point, ggplot, theme_minimal

dat = load_penguins().dropna()

(
    ggplot(dat, aes(x="bill_depth_mm", y="body_mass_g", color="species"))
    + geom_point(alpha=0.7)
    + theme_minimal()
)

ui.input_select("x", "", num_cols, selected="bill_depth_mm")

1@render.plot

2        ggplot(dat, aes(x=input.x(), y=input.y(), color="species"))

title: Your First Application
text:
What just happened?

input.x() and input.y() are reactive values — they change when the user picks
@render.plot creates a reactive context — it re-runs whenever its inputs change
Both dropdowns drive the same output with no extra wiring

Shiny tracked both dependencies automatically.

title: Your First Application
text:
The complete app


code/app-01-intro.py

from palmerpenguins import load_penguins
from plotnine import aes, geom_point, ggplot, theme_minimal
from shiny.express import input, render, ui

dat = load_penguins().dropna()
num_cols = dat.select_dtypes("float64").columns.tolist()

ui.input_select("x", "", num_cols, selected="bill_depth_mm")
ui.input_select("y", "", num_cols, selected="body_mass_g")


@render.plot
def plot():
    return (ggplot(dat, aes(x=input.x(), y=input.y(), color="species")) +
        geom_point(alpha=0.7) +
        theme_minimal()
    )

title: Daniel Chen
text:
Hi I’m Daniel. I’m a Lecturer at the University of British Columbia. I primary teach Data Science tooling through the Statistics department, and through the Master’s of Data Science program at UBC.
Previously, I was a data science educator and developer advocate at Posit, PBC. They are the authors of a TON of open source data science tools, some of which are used in this tutorial. They’ll be in the PyCon US 2026 Expo hall (booth 539), so check them out!

title: Daniel Chen
text:
How it’s made
If you’re curious about the tools about how this tutorial site was made:

Quarto Website + Quarto Slides

https://quarto.org/

Shinylive = Shiny + WebAssembly

https://shiny.posit.co/py/get-started/shinylive.html
Shinylive + quarto:

https://github.com/quarto-ext/shinylive
https://quarto-ext.github.io/shinylive/


brand.yml: Specifying colors and branding

https://posit-dev.github.io/brand-yml/

title: Resources
text:
Shiny for Python docs: https://shiny.posit.co/py/
Component gallery: https://shiny.posit.co/py/components/
Templates: https://shiny.posit.co/py/templates/
chatlas: https://posit-dev.github.io/chatlas/
shinychat: https://posit-dev.github.io/shinychat/py/
querychat: https://github.com/posit-dev/querychat
